data.title: Brown-Forman: A Deal With Pernod Ricard Could Be A Game Changer
data.description:
The industry-wide headwinds within the spirits sector have decimated all major players within the industry and are now spurring deals that a couple of years ago would have seemed unlikely.

Discussions about a potential acquisition of Brown-Forman (BF.B) by Pernod Ricard (PRNDY) are now being held, and this could create a truly global brand powerhouse in the sector that will rival the current leader, Diageo (DEO).

All three names mentioned above are down by more than 60% over the past 5 years, which shows the severity of the current downturn that has likely facilitated the ongoing talks.

Additionally, the fact that both BF.B and PRNDY are down significantly over that period, and trading at relatively comparable multiples means that a share-based acquisition would not hurt BF.B investors when it comes to their future share within the combined entity.

Therefore, the more important questions right now are how accretive a potential deal would be for both companies and what the potential benefits and risks involved are.

A Major Rival Of Diageo

Right off the bat, a potential acquisition would allow the combined entity to rival the current hegemon in the sector—Diageo. In terms of revenues, it would still be smaller than DEO, but in a much better position to take advantage of economies of scale.

In terms of valuation multiples, BF.B now trades at a notable premium to both DEO and PRNDY. The difference is noticeable both on EV/EBITDA (see the graph above) and on a forward price/earnings basis (see the graph below).

While this is good news for BF.B shareholders, it should be noted that the company was trading at significantly higher multiples relative to PRNDY just a couple of years ago. That means that Brown-Forman's management is obliged to negotiate a higher target price for the company.

Synergies

The word synergies is often used left and right when a potential acquisition or a merger is being discussed. Quite often, however, promises of cost savings turn sour for shareholders, especially in industries with a high relative share of intangible assets.

As a heavily branded sector, the spirits industry is definitely a good example of an area where tangible assets are of relatively low importance and potential synergies would be questionable.

Nonetheless, a combination between BF.B and PRNDY would be very attractive from a synergy point of view for a number of reasons.

First, both companies have selling, general & administrative expenses to sales ratios of around 32% to 33%. The share of fixed costs to sales has been gradually falling over the years as companies grew, but this process has been very slow. On the other hand, Diageo, as we saw above, is much larger in terms of revenues, and has an SG&A-to-sales ratio of 18%.

This gap between BF.B and PRNDY on one hand and DEO on the other show the ballpark figure of potential economies of scale and cost savings if post-acquisition restructuring is done right. Of course, investors should be mindful of the fact that this is a tedious process that is usually associated with some increased up-front restructuring costs.

The case for realizing these enormous synergies is strengthened further by the geographical exposure of the two companies. Brown-Forman, for example, generates roughly half of its sales from the United States, and its Jack Daniel's brand is one of the most well-known in the country. Woodford Reserve, on the other hand, is well-positioned in the premium segment.

The space occupied by Jack Daniel's and Woodford Reserve is an area where PRDNY is weak when it comes to its large strategic international brands. Here PRNDY has iconic vodka, gin, rum, Scotch, and Irish whiskey brands, but it lacks an American whiskey brand that is comparable to those owned by Brown-Forman.

Pernod Ricard would also benefit from Brown-Forman's distribution network in the United States, where it was lagging and struggling to close the gap with peers.

The ready-to-drink category is also an area where the two companies would be complementary to each other, as Brown-Forman had huge success with its Jack Daniel's Ready-to-Drink, while PRDNY has been gearing for growth with its non-alcoholic and RTD offerings.

Overall, the two companies are an almost perfect fit when it comes to geographical and product segmentation, which, in my view, significantly strengthens the case for notable synergies to be realized.

One potential issue that investors would need to be aware of is the cultural aspect of the two businesses. As an American family-controlled entity, Brown-Forman differs from PRDNY, which is a French company that is structured around the founding Ricard family. While the cultural aspect is very subjective to analyze, and I would abstain from making any generalizations in that regard, investors should be aware that a potential combination would not be as smooth as merging two entities from the country.

Investor Takeaway

In a nutshell, it is difficult to argue against a possible combination between BF.B and PRNDY. The two companies are an almost perfect fit when it comes to geographical and product segmentation. It is still too early to draw any solid conclusions about a potential deal, and more information is needed, but a combined entity could be a serious threat for one of my personal favorites in the sector—Diageo. As more details about the acquisition emerge, investors would need to be mindful of the target price that would be put on BF.B. as it used to trade at even higher multiples to PRNDY. Lastly, the cultural aspect of the two companies should not be ignored, as it could easily create some bumps down the road.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nvidia Won't Be Dead Money For Much Longer
data.description:
Near the end of last year, I upgraded Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) to a Strong Buy rating for the first time. Citing fundamental strengthening, solid guidance, and valuation contraction, I believed the stock was in a strong position to deliver outperformance.

So far, that hasn't played out. Since the publication of that piece, Nvidia has actually seen modest declines, and overall, the stock has really been dead money for around half a year now. With their latest results having come out in late February and the GTC happening not long ago, I have decided to provide a much-needed update for readers.

Below, it is shown that Nvidia's fundamentals have continued to strengthen. Despite some risks moving forward, solid guidance and new opportunities show that the long-term prospects remain bright. Meanwhile, the valuation has seen steep contraction, and the forward P/E is now at a discount to the sector. Nvidia stock has undeniably been dead money for an extended period, but given the current setup, I would say it is likely to wake up sooner rather than later. Therefore, I'm reiterating my Strong Buy rating.

Growth Rebound Continues

We'll start off today with an overview of their latest numbers to see how their financial performance has been recently. For FY2026 Q4, Nvidia generated record revenues of $68.1 billion, up an impressive 73.21% YoY. As you can see in the chart above, top-line growth has now accelerated for a second straight quarter to confirm that an inflection point has indeed happened. CEO Jensen Huang stated that

Computing demand is growing exponentially — the agentic AI inflection point has arrived. Grace Blackwell with NVLink is the king of inference today — delivering an order-of-magnitude lower cost per token — and Vera Rubin will extend that leadership even further.

In my view, the considerable rebound in growth after an extended period of deceleration shows that Huang's observations are correct and that this robust demand is translating directly into financial growth for the company. Just recently, there have been further signals that demand will continue to be solid. Earlier this month, Elon Musk stated that SpaceX (SPACE), xAI (X.AI), and Tesla (TSLA) will keep purchasing Nvidia chips at scale, and Amazon (AMZN) AWS also committed to buying another 1 million chips by the end of 2027.

Lastly, it should be noted that Nvidia beat analyst expectations by $1.9 billion to extend their streak of outperformance. All in all, I would say that top-line results show business activity continues to be extraordinarily strong for the company, as demand has no signs of softening at this point.

Don't Overlook Networking

For their all-important data center business, results were impressive given how large their growth base is. They reported total revenues in this segment of $62.3 billion, up 75% YoY to outpace the company-wide top-line growth rate. This shows that data center solutions continue to be a growth leader for Nvidia. That said, while state-of-the-art GPUs like Blackwell Ultra get all the buzz in the media, the growth we have seen in networking is exceptional.

As you can see above, compute revenues grew by a very respectable 57%, but note that networking revenues soared by 267%. While compute is, of course, still the much larger business, investors shouldn't overlook the potential and importance of their networking solutions. In their earnings presentation, they stated that they now have the "world’s largest networking business" and given their growth rate, I would say they are in a position to extend their lead. Overall, I believe it is clear that their key data center segment continues to see very robust momentum.

Profitability Improves

In addition to rebounding revenue growth, profitability continues to move in the right direction despite earlier fears that strong margins can't be kept up forever. For gross margin, there were 1.7 percentage points of expansion YoY, and so Nvidia has controlled costs well. They attributed this strength to lower inventory provisions.

As for operating expenses, there was a notable 51% increase YoY due to investments for future growth. However, since this is considerably lower than the revenue growth rate, operating efficiency has actually improved, and operating margin has, of course, expanded as well.

This came through to the bottom line as they saw a nice 82% jump in their EPS. This is an acceleration of growth as well, and they also beat expectations here. As a whole, the profitability of the company is in an increasingly strong position, and of course that is an encouraging sign.

Q1 Guidance

Before talking about some opportunities and risks moving forward, let's take a look at their financial guidance for FY2027 Q1. At the midpoint, Nvidia sees revenues of $78 billion, and that would represent a 77% YoY growth rate. Since a 73% increase was reported in Q4, the company is now on track to see a third consecutive quarter of growth acceleration. This indicates Nvidia is regaining momentum for their business trajectory, and it should be noted that this top-line guidance was also above consensus.

For gross margin, they are projecting 75% at the midpoint. This would be a clear improvement from the year-ago quarter's gross margin (excluding the H2O charge) of 71.3%, and so cost control will remain strong. Lastly, they see around $7.5 billion in operating expenses. That is a bit high, as a 109% jump is implied, but given that they're continuing to invest in the future of the business, I would say that this is not a major concern at this point. In my view, Q1 guidance is solid and suggests continued fundamental strength moving forward.

Opportunities

The first major opportunity moving forward would be the reopening of the Chinese market to their chips. On March 17th, CEO Huang stated that the company had received orders from China and that they were working on restarting manufacturing. Just a day later, there were reports that the Chinese Government had approved the H200 for sale. While there is, of course, competition from domestic companies like Huawei, the reintroduction of their chips in a large market should help to drive growth. Furthermore, plans to sell Groq chips there could further boost their prospects in the country. Just recently, Wells Fargo (WFC) expressed their view that China could add $25 billion or more to Nvidia's annual revenue, and so the positive developments recently are a big deal.

With SpaceX planning to go public later this year to raise capital for space data centers, it is becoming increasingly obvious that there is a growing opportunity in this area. For Nvidia, their quick move to release the Space-1 Vera Rubin Module at GTC 2026 shows that they are adapting to the needs of the market without hesitation. While it is much too early to quantify how big the opportunity and market really are, I would say that this new frontier for data centers offers Nvidia another potential growth driver in the coming years and beyond.

Risks

A first risk that should be noted is their Groq deal. Their agreement was non-exclusive licensing for their inference technology and not an acquisition, but it is still drawing some antitrust concerns. Around a week ago, two senators reportedly wrote to Nvidia to inquire about this deal. Questions included whether the transaction was structured this way to avoid antitrust scrutiny. While there doesn't seem to have been formal investigations, I would say that their Groq deal may continue to draw some form of unwanted attention moving forward.

The much larger risk that Nvidia has to deal with is basically competition from their customers. While this isn't breaking news, it should be noted that hyperscalers are continuing to develop their own chips to reduce reliance on Nvidia. Earlier this month, Wedbush Securities stated that Google (GOOGL), Amazon, and Meta (META) are all "aggressively focused" on building alternatives, and so over time, Nvidia's chips may see an erosion in overall market share. The versatility of GPUs like Blackwell Ultra means that there will always be a large market for these chips, but the continued rise of ASICs could nonetheless lower their growth trajectory over time.

Valuation Is Very Cheap

As you can see in the above valuation chart, Nvidia's forward P/E ratio has contracted to a reading of just 20.41. The recent sharp drop is obviously due to a revision of growth targets by analysts, but in my view, the risk/reward is now extremely attractive for the bulls.

In today's article, it was shown that Nvidia's fundamentals have continued to strengthen. Accelerating growth and expanding margin are a dream combo, and recall that guidance for FY2027 Q1 was also solid. Just above, it was shown that there are both opportunities and risks for Nvidia, but given the momentum they are seeing now, I would say that the former outweighs the latter at this point.

With the long-term picture in many ways stronger than it was just a few months ago, I would say that the forward P/E's currently depressed level shows that the market is underappreciating what Nvidia brings to the table. Funny enough, their non-GAAP forward P/E is now at a discount to the IT sector, according to Seeking Alpha, and so the stock is indeed very cheap at current levels.

Conclusion

Nvidia stock has been trading essentially sideways now for quite a while. Even though it has been dead money for months, I believe the current setup shows that it could wake up in the not-so-distant future. Strengthening fundamentals, a bright outlook, and a dirt-cheap valuation are key ingredients to stock outperformance, and Nvidia ticks every one of these boxes. The risk/reward has further improved in favor of the bulls since my previous article, and so I have decided to maintain my strong buy rating on the stock at this juncture.

data.title: Maze Therapeutics: MZE829 Presses Forward Despite Stock Price Drop
data.description:
I'm initiating coverage of Maze Therapeutics (MAZE) with a "Buy" rating. Just a few days ago, the company reported proof-of-concept [POC] data from its phase 2 HORIZON study using its dual-mechanism APOL1 inhibitor MZE829 for the treatment of patients with APOL1-mediated kidney disease [AMKD]. It was noted that 12 patients given this drug over a 12-week period achieved a clinically meaningful reduction in proteinuria of 35.6% as measured by urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio [uACR]. Despite this, the stock price dropped by 37% despite having reported this positive data. It is not clear why the stock price dropped; maybe investors were expecting more, but the company had met the bar it set out, having shown at least a 30% uACR reduction or better. One possible reason is because a competitor by the name of Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) is also in the process of the development of its drug inaxaplin as an APOL1 inhibitor for the treatment of patients with APOL1-mediated kidney disease [AMKD].

More about this below, but Vertex achieved a greater protein reduction for its phase 2a study; however, I don't believe that the trials should be compared because this study was focused on treating a subset of AMKD patients with focal segmental glomerulosclerosis [FSGS]. I believe that the stock price trading lower was a huge overreaction and that this stock remains a "Buy" rating. The company even did well in targeting the FSGS subpopulation as well, providing a greater reduction of proteinuria. The truth is that this program is not ending; therefore, the drop doesn't make much sense. As a matter of fact, it already has plans to initiate a pivotal program using MZE829 to treat AMKD patients. The risk is that Vertex is advancing its phase 2/3 AMPLITUDE study using inaxaplin to treat AMKD patients [broad population].

It is expected to release interim data from this study either by the end of 2026 or early 2027 [Vertex Pharmaceuticals Q4 and Full-year 2025 earnings Presentation February 12, 2026, slide #7]. Lastly, there are additional shots on goal with this company. It already saw positive signals in phase 1 human testing for its SLC6A19 inhibitor MZE782 for the treatment of patients with Phenylketonuria [PKU]. With that said, it is initiating two POC studies in 2026 for this program. One phase 2 study is going to attempt to achieve plasma phenylalanine [Phe] reduction in PKU, and then the other phase 2 study is going to look at proteinuria reduction in chronic kidney disease [CKD] patients. With the ability for this company to press forward towards a pivotal program of MZE829 in AMKD patients, plus additional shots on goal with its SLC6A19 inhibitor MZE782 for PKU and CKD, I believe that this stock should be initiated with a "Buy" rating. The reason for not a "Strong Buy" rating yet is because there remains the risk of the Vertex program in place, which could potentially overshadow MZE829, but it remains to be seen.

MZE829 Data Delivers in AMKD And Stock Plunge Is Huge Overblown Reaction

As I noted above, Maze Therapeutics released data from its phase 2 proof-of-concept [POC] HORIZON study. This mid-stage trial used the company's APOL1 inhibitor MZE829 for the treatment of patients with APOL1-mediated kidney disease [AMKD] The beauty of this trial was that it was set as an open-label basket design trial. This allowed the company to recruit a broad group of AMKD patients, including those who were diabetic, non-diabetic, and non-diabetic patients with FSGS. Before going over all of the clinical data released from this HORIZON study, plus any catalysts to come about for investors to keep an eye on, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

APOL1-mediated kidney disease [AMKD] is characterized as a genetic kidney disorder wherein, in order for a person to get this, they must inherit two risk variants [one from each parent]. These two risk variants are G1 and/or G2 of the APOL1 gene. When this gene is functioning as a normal one, it's responsible for producing a protein that helps the person's immune system. In the case of AMKD patients, there are mutations of this gene, and it causes two items to come about:

  • Damage occurs to parts of the kidney responsible for filtering out blood
  • Cell death in the kidney, which leads to inflammation and scarring

One important factor to note is that not everyone that inherits two copies of an APOL1 risk variant ends up having AMKD. It is believed that even if this actually happens, only about between 15% and 20% have a chance of developing kidney disease. In addition, there is something else to mention about this disorder, which is that if one inherits both copies of the APOL1 gene, there is a 10x likelihood of them getting a disorder known as Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis [FSGS]. With this kidney disorder, there is involvement of scarring of the filtering units of the kidney. I brought this up because the company actually had evaluated a subset of these FSGS patients from the phase 2 HORIZON study. AMKD is a subset of Chronic Kidney Disease [CKD] and is observed primarily in people of African ancestry. The reason why is because the mutation is responsible for a factor of protecting from a parasite that causes African sleeping sickness, but the mutation also causes kidney damage in some. Why does there need to be a disease-modifying therapy like MZE829? That's because there are no currently available targeted therapies for these patients for starters. Also, this subset of CKD patients actually progresses in disease faster than those of non-APOL1-mediated kidney disease. The reason why is because their faster disease progression results in them initiating dialysis 10 years earlier than CKD patients. With AMKD patients, they can suffer from high blood pressure, protein in the urine [proteinuria], fatigue, and swelling of the legs. The most important measure being proteinuria, which the company released with its results from the phase 2 HORIZON study.

In order to see whether or not its APOL1 inhibitor would be effective in being able to treat these patients with APOL1-mediated kidney disease [AMKD], it ran the phase 2 HORIZON trial. This mid-stage study recruited up to a total of 15 patients for both safety and tolerability analysis. However, in terms of evaluation of the efficacy outcome using MZE829, only 12 patients were evaluable. Being that this was an open-label basket trial design, it was laid out to recruit the following, which I mentioned above:

  • APOL1-mediated CKD patients with diabetes
  • APOL1-mediated CKD patients without diabetes
  • Severe FSGS without diabetes

The 12 patients in question, across all these subpopulations, were given 250 mg oral MZE829 over a 12-week period. The final outcome was that all patients achieved a 35.6% mean reduction in proteinuria as measured by urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio [uACR]. Now, for the first reason why I believe that the stock dropping on data was overblown. The threshold the company set out was to achieve an average of at least a ≥30% uACR reduction [Maze Therapeutics HORIZON phase 2 topline data Corporate Presentation March 25, 2026, slide #10]. Why is this important? That's because this is the threshold set out in order for these AMKD patients to both achieve a 10-year delay to End-Stage-Kidney-Disease [ESKD] and achieve an eGFR slope improvement as well. The second reason goes to that of what I noted above about Vertex Pharmaceuticals with inaxaplin, which is focused on targeting CKD patients with two APOL1 variants with FSGS. Again, it has the ongoing phase 2/3 AMPLITUDE study to assess the effect of inaxaplin on kidney function and proteinuria in patients with AMKD. This is a risk because if it releases a positive interim analysis for this patient population by either end of 2026 or early 2027, then it will be compared to all other available data to date [including that of HORIZON]. However, the prior data that Vertex released was that of the inaxaplin effect on proteinuria of patients with two APOL1 variants and FSGS. For this subpopulation of patients, the change in baseline of uACR at week 13 was 47.6%. The outcome for the 4 FSGS patients who received MZE829 in terms of mean uACR reduction at week 12 was said to be that of 61.8%.

The point here is that it is a small subset of patients, but this is a good starting point. Plus, it is not always ideal to do cross-trial comparisons. Still, the drop in stock price doesn't make sense from my perspective. The company reached the ≥30% uACR reduction threshold it needed for the whole patient population of AMKD patients and did better in FSGS patients [albeit a small sample size] than Vertex's inaxaplin study. Based on everything I laid out above, plus the fact that the data is good to move forward with for a pivotal study program for MZE829, it means that this program is still strong. I believe that that stock market reaction was overblown. Had this trial failed to reach the ≥30% uACR reduction threshold, then I would understand the reason for the drop, as it wouldn't have been a meaningful outcome in that case.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Maze Therapeutics had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $360 million as of December 31, 2025. The reason for the cash on hand is because the company was able to first obtain a total of $150 million before deducting expenses from an oversubscribed private placement agreement back in September of 2025. The truth is that this company has a lot of cash on hand. What makes me say that? Well, the truth is that there are several reasons why.

The first and most notable reason is the cash runway guidance it has given. It believes that it has enough cash on hand to fund its operations into 2028. Secondly, it actually has a 2026 ATM Open Market Sales Agreement with Jefferies LLC that it can use [established in February 2026], whereby it could from time to time offer and sell up to an aggregate of $200 million. To date, thus far, no shares have been sold under this agreement. The last reason is because it entered into a senior secured term loan facility with Hercules Capital Incorporated for an aggregate principal amount of $200 million.

As part of developing this term loan facility, Maze Therapeutics received an initial term loan amount of $40 million. There is potential to earn up to an additional six term loan tranches for up to an aggregate of $160 million through February 2031. However, in order to receive these other term loan tranches, there are certain conditions that have to be met. The company burns about $38.1 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down to $27.6 million in R&D expenses and then $10.5 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Maze Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the positive data that the company just obtained from its phase 2 HORIZON study using MZE829 for the treatment of patients with AMKD. The reason why is because the company intends to take the data it obtained thus far and initiate a pivotal program. The risk here is that even though the company established POC data showing a mean uACR reduction of 35.6% from the trial, there is no assurance that the FDA is going to allow the company to initiate a phase 3 clinical study using this drug to treat these AMKD patients.

The second risk lies with the fact that the company is not the only one developing an APOL1 inhibitor for the treatment of patients with AMKD. As I have shown above, Vertex Pharmaceuticals is in the process of evaluating the use of the small molecule inhibitor of APOL1, inaxaplin, in the ongoing phase 2/3 AMPLITUDE study to treat patients with AMKD. Vertex believes it is able to release 48-week interim results from this study either by the end of 2026 or early 2027. The risk here is that its drug could achieve a significantly better mean proteinuria reduction compared to what has been observed so far with MZE829. Thus, not only does Maze have to do well on its own in a potential phase 3 study, but its data is likely to be compared to that of the AMPLITUDE study from Vertex.

The third and final risk would be in terms of the additional shots on goal that this company has as part of its pipeline. Its other candidate, SLC6A19 inhibitor MZE782, is to be explored in two separate POC studies treating patients with PKU and CKD. Both of these mid-stage POC trials are expected to be initiated in 2026. The risk here is that even though MZE782 was able to achieve an initial eGFR dip of -11.5 mL/min/1.73 m^2 over seven days versus -2.5 for placebo for patients that took 240 mg of this drug twice daily, there is no guarantee that this will be carried over for an extended period of time in these other mid-stage trials.

Conclusion

I believe it's important to initiate Maze Therapeutics with a "Buy" rating for now. The reason why is because one can't say that the data was very bad. The company met the necessary threshold from its phase 2 HORIZON trial using MZE829 for the treatment of patients with AMKD. Again, the threshold was just to observe a mean uACR reduction of ≥30%. The company exceeded this expectation easily, and this is the first reason. Secondly, it's notable that despite only 4 FSGS patients being treated with MZE829, the drug did perform better than that of Vertex's study.

However, it's not prudent to do cross-trial comparisons anyways, but this gives an idea of where this drug is at in terms of targeting other subpopulations of AMKD patients. I still believe that the selloff on the stock was overblown. Maze not only did what it needed to, but it could also be in line to be able to initiate a pivotal late-stage study for this program of MZE829 treating these patients with AMKD. With everything I mentioned above, I'm initiating this stock with a "Buy" rating. Should Vertex produce significantly superior data, or should something change with the progress of MZE829, then I believe that it would be important to change the rating at such time.

data.title: EPS: A Quality ETF Based On An Earnings-Weighted Approach, But A Hold For Now
data.description:
With an investment structure that favors companies with large and high-margin businesses, the WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap Fund ETF (EPS) is arguably a fund with a quality tilt. This is a core characteristic of the fund that is reflected in its increased exposure to technology and communication services, especially big tech, if we take as a reference my previous article covering this fund from late 2024. Although EPS’s current portfolio may hold up well in the short term, supported by the financial strength and business resilience of its core holdings, ongoing uncertainties do not help, warranting a cautious stance at this moment. With that in mind, the coming sections will break down the fund’s portfolio and compare sector allocation, total returns, and volatility with the broader market and a peer group of ETFs.

ETF Description And Highlights

EPS is designed to provide exposure to profitable companies in the U.S. equity market. Using a rules-based methodology, the fund first filters out companies that have not generated positive earnings in each of the past four quarters. From this pool, the largest 500 companies by market cap are then weighted proportionally according to their earnings, subject to certain liquidity thresholds and a minimum price-to-earnings ratio of at least 2x, and with annual rebalances.

As of March 11, 2026, EPS had $1.3 billion in assets under management across a portfolio of 502 companies, with an average market capitalization of $410.1 billion. As much as a market-cap index like the Russell 1000, the fund shows a clear tilt toward the upper end of the market cap spectrum, with mega caps representing 46.1% of total assets and large caps 35.2%, which together account for more than 80% of the fund. This has changed little compared to the picture in October 2024. Still, there has been a small increase in the allocation to mega caps from 43.4% to 46.1%, while exposure to small caps dropped from 21.7% to 17.9%. This slight change makes sense, given the strong earnings growth delivered by mega caps over the last two years, especially big tech, which has led earnings growth in the equity market for much of the past few years. Thus, an increased allocation to mega caps is somewhat expected for an earnings-weighted fund like EPS.

EPS’s top ten holdings are NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) (7.3%), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (6.0%), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) (5.6%), Apple Inc. (AAPL) (5.1%), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) (4.1%), Meta Platforms (3.7%), JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) (2.0%), Berkshire Hathaway, Inc. (BRK.B) (1.9%), Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO) (1.6%), and Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) (1.5%), resembling to a large extent the holdings of broader equity indexes like the Russell 1000, where there is a large presence of giants from technology and communication services, given their strong earnings profile.

The following table includes a list of six ETFs blending value-oriented approaches and investment strategies driven by fundamentals, which will serve as the peer group for EPS in the analysis. The first is the Vanguard Value Index Fund ETF (VTV), by far the largest in the group, which tracks the CRSP US Large Cap Value Index with a traditional market-cap-weighted approach. A similar philosophy can be seen in the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD) and the iShares S&P 500 Value ETF (IVE), both relying on market-cap weighting while screening for value characteristics, with IWD leaning more into mid caps (30%). Employing a different angle, the Schwab Fundamental U.S. Large Company ETF (FNDX) and the Invesco RAFI US 1000 ETF (PRF) rely on fundamental metrics such as sales, cash flow, and dividends, and the VanEck Morningstar Wide Moat ETF (MOAT) tracks an equal-weighted index constituted of companies with sustainable competitive advantages and attractive valuations.

EPS’s largest sector is technology, which accounts for 31.3% of the fund. A second tier includes financial services (15.5%) and communication services (13.3%), healthcare (10.6%), and consumer discretionary (10.3%), while smaller allocations are spread across industrials (5.5%), consumer staples (4.6%), energy (4.4%), utilities (2.2%), basic materials (1.4%), and real estate (0.9%).

The fund has increased its allocation to technology by 4.8% since late 2024, in a sign of the stronger earnings growth from companies in the sector. At the same time, sectors like energy, industrials, consumer staples, and financial services had their weights reduced by the fund, as the fund needs to rebalance holdings to reflect its earnings-weighted structure. That said, EPS’s current sector mix has much in common with broader equity indexes like the Russell 1000, with only small allocation gaps in sectors like financial services (+3.2%), communication services (+2.8%), and industrials (-3.8%).

If we break down EPS’s holdings across its core sectors, we see that in technology, although the fund has nearly 75 positions in the sector, the bulk of the exposure is concentrated in a few names. Nvidia is by far the largest semiconductor player, followed by Broadcom, Applied Materials, Inc. (AMAT), and Qualcomm. Apple and Microsoft are other large holdings with more than 9% of the fund, while the exposure to software is complemented by smaller positions in Salesforce, Inc. (CRM), Intuit Inc. (INTU), and Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR), along with cybersecurity vendor Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW).

The exposure to financial services also includes more than 70 companies but is anchored by a few sub-industries, such as global banks through JPMorgan, Bank of America Corporation (BAC), Wells Fargo & Company (WFC), and Citigroup Inc. (C). The fund also owns the conglomerate Berkshire Hathaway and global payments players Visa, Inc. (V) and Mastercard Incorporated (MA), while holdings in insurance, such as The Allstate Corporation (ALL), MetLife, Inc. (MET), and Prudential Financial, Inc. (PRU), round out the sector.

Another large sector is communication services, but with only 16 holdings and heavy positions in Alphabet and Meta Platforms, two companies with sizable earnings. Elsewhere, there is some diversification through telecom services providers AT&T, Inc. (T), Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ), and Comcast, in addition to positions in entertainment, including The Walt Disney Company (DIS) and Fox Corporation (FOX).

Outside the top three sectors, the fund has significant exposure to consumer discretionary, mainly through Amazon, with marginal allocations to the travel, auto, and home improvement segments. Beyond that, the portfolio still has positions in energy through Exxon Mobil and Chevron Corporation (CVX), while the allocation to healthcare is spread more evenly across names like Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK), Pfizer Inc. (PFE), UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH), and biotechnology names Amgen Inc. (AMGN) and Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD).

In summary, given its earnings-weighted methodology, the EPS holds a portfolio centered on companies that generate large earnings, which naturally tilts it toward large and mega caps, and especially big tech names, like NVIDIA, Alphabet, Amazon, and Meta Platforms. Given that these giants remain among the companies with the most consistent earnings growth across the equity market, my read is that these names are likely to increase their weights in the fund over time. Therefore, EPS should show a stronger mega-cap and big-tech tilt from here, which is not bad, given the uncertainties created by geopolitical tensions and a slower growth scenario. The flip side, however, is the heavy exposure to these big tech names, which have made huge investments to build their AI infrastructure. Time will tell whether such investments will pay off as expected.

Given the value and fundamentally driven approach of the peer group, it is not surprising that EPS is heavily overweight in technology, as all peers have less than 20% allocated to the sector. But the fund is also overweight in consumer discretionary and communication services, given the peers’ cautious view on above-average valuations in these two sectors. In contrast, EPS is underweight in industrials, consumer staples, and several other sectors, including those with both cyclical and defensive profiles, as peers maintain a relatively balanced approach, though tilted toward the conservative side, as opposed to EPS’s methodology that aims to maximize earnings across the portfolio.

As a result, EPS’s sector correlation remains modest at the 0.50 to 0.70 range with most peers. The exceptions are FNDX (0.86) and MOAT (0.92), whose fundamental approaches give more weight to technology and communication services, where names like big techs are well known for their financial strength and business resilience.

Modest Valuation Discount

At 19.6x P/E, EPS trades at about a 10% discount to the Russell 1000’s 21.9x. This is mostly driven by stock selection in sectors like consumer discretionary and industrials that have average P/E multiples nearly 40% to 50% lower than those of the Russell 1000. At the same time, sectors like technology and financial services also trade below the benchmark, though within a much narrower 5%-10% range. Key factors driving valuation down include the absence of positions in high-multiple names like Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), The Boeing Company (BA), and Cloudflare, Inc. (NET), along with overweight positions in banks, Pfizer, and automakers such as General Motors Company (GM) and Ford Motor Company (F).

EPS compares reasonably well with the Russell 1000 from a growth perspective. The fund’s 10.5% historical earnings growth is ahead of the benchmark’s 8.7%, while sales growth of 7.8% also exceeds the index’s 7.0%. The strongest driver is clearly the technology sector, where EPS shows stronger growth than the benchmark. At the individual stock level, key contributors include the fund's greater exposure to names like Nvidia, Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta Platforms.

The fund shows profitability metrics that are broadly in line with the Russell 1000. The fund’s 35.8% EBITDA margin is 1.4% higher than the benchmark’s 34.4%, while its 40.9% ROE is slightly below the Russell 1000’s 41.3%. Of course, much of this comes from EPS’s sector mix, which is admittedly close to the index. On balance, better margins in technology and communication services offset weaker metrics in industrials, financial services, and energy, thanks to overweight exposure to big techs like Nvidia, Meta Platforms, and Alphabet.

Overall, what we can see here is a fund that has a high-quality bias, given its above-average profitability metrics, thanks to its large exposure to big tech in general. With EPS keeping its allocation profile from here, the fund is positioned as a quality player in the ETF universe. With the outlook still clouded by the ongoing Middle East conflict, oil prices continue to pose a risk for inflation and economic growth. Thus, EPS seems well-positioned to navigate an extended period of weaker sentiment for the stock market as a whole, likely outperforming the broader market, given its below-average volatility profile, as we will see later in this article.

EPS sits at the higher end of the valuation range, alongside IVE (19.7x P/E), driven by its greater allocation to large and mega caps at nearly 67% compared to other peers. Meanwhile, all other peers trade at below 18.5x P/E, a discount relative to EPS and the Russell 1000 that stems largely from their underweight exposure to technology and consumer discretionary. Indeed, EPS’s premium valuation versus the peer group is also consistent with the fund’s heavier allocation to large and mega caps, as these market cap categories make up as much as 82%, as opposed to most peers, where they only represent 50% to 70%, and lower-multiple small and mid caps take a greater portion of their portfolio. In addition, EPS’s historically stronger earnings growth profile appears to justify its valuation premium. That said, considering EPS’s portfolio is expected to remain anchored in large and mega caps, as discussed earlier, the fund should continue to trade at least at a small premium to most peers going forward, including those that follow an investment approach based on companies’ fundamentals, as these funds usually incorporate valuation metrics in their stock selection approach.

Above-Average Performance Relative To Peers

With annualized returns of 19.4% (3-year) and 11.8% (5-year), EPS has slightly underperformed the Russell 1000, with a price action that has seen a strong correlation with the index, with the exception of 2020, when the fund delivered much worse gains. But EPS has outpaced the peer group over time, with the best performance in the 3-year period and the third-best performance in the 5-year window. This, of course, has much to do with the peer group’s conservative exposure to growth sectors like technology and consumer discretionary in a market that has been generally bullish over the past years, despite the selloffs, such as we have experienced recently.

EPS’s price moves have been a bit more contained than the Russell 1000, as illustrated by its beta in the 0.94-0.95 range. And most of that seems tied to the fund’s more modest performance during rallies, as it has not kept up with the Russell 1000, as indicated by its upside capture ratio of 97. Still, the fund has followed the index more closely than peers in risk-on environments, since their upside capture ratios have been in the 83-95 range for most funds. This helps explain EPS’s outperformance over peers, particularly in positive years like 2023 to 2025. On the other hand, the peer group’s volatility has been lower than EPS, with betas below 0.90, with the exception of MOAT, driven by their more defensive sector mix, with overweight exposure to consumer staples, healthcare, and utilities.

EPS’s average daily volume of $4.12 million is light compared with most peers, which trade well above $100 million a day. This is reflected in its 0.06% bid-ask spread that is not that high but is wider than the peer group’s 0.02% average. The fund’s turnover of 16% is well below peers, which is consistent with a more stable large- and mega-cap allocation strategy. This supports a very competitive expense ratio of 0.08%, the second-lowest in the group. Meanwhile, the fund offers a 12-month yield of 1.25%, which is somewhat below the group, as peers have greater allocation to above-average yielding sectors like healthcare, utilities, and consumer staples. On the other hand, EPS’s tracking error in the 0.62%-0.78% range is in line with most peers and is consistent with a rules-based allocation strategy. Finally, its Sharpe ratio of 1.31 over three years is the highest in the group, while its 0.72 over five years sits at the higher end of the range, along with VTV, FNDX, and PRF. This suggests that EPS has maintained above-average risk-adjusted returns over time, boosted by a strong outperformance in the 3-year period, despite a slightly higher volatility than most peers.

Final Thoughts

EPS’s earnings-weighted structure results in a portfolio tilted toward large and profitable companies in the U.S. market. The fund’s large exposure to mega caps from technology and communication services helps explain a quality-growth profile, as so-called big techs are typically high-margin businesses and continue to deliver above-average earnings growth. Still, its valuation is slightly below the Russell 1000, driven by holdings in low-multiple names that offset premium multiples seen in the technology sector. This profile is expected to offer some degree of stability in an environment where macro risks tied to oil prices and mixed economic data cloud the short-term outlook, as earnings in the fund’s core holdings are expected to be resilient to a certain extent.

The flip side, though, is that in addition to this macro backdrop, the beginning of this year has also been marked by growing concerns about large investments made by companies like Alphabet, Amazon, and Meta Platforms in their AI infrastructure. That paints a mixed scenario for these companies and the broader market, and of course, EPS is not immune to it either. Thus, while the fund remains well-positioned in a market that is likely to reward earnings visibility, current macro uncertainties warrant caution, at least in the short term, which underscores my neutral stance on this fund at this moment. In spite of that, current shareholders are expected to benefit from a potential upside that I project to be in the high-single-digit to low-double-digit range over the next twelve months, driven by earnings growth expectations that remain in the double-digits for broader equity indexes in 2026.

data.title: Autolus Therapeutics: Q4 Earnings Highlight Importance Of Autoimmune Data Catalysts
data.description:
Investment Overview

Autolus Therapeutics plc (AUTL), the London, United Kingdom and Gaithersburg, Maryland headquartered, commercial stage biotech company, marketer and seller of Aucatzyl (obecabtagene autoleucel), a cell therapy indicated for adults with relapsed or refractory B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia, announced its Q4 and full-year 2025 earnings and business updates earlier today. At the time of writing, the stock is down ~5% for the day, trading at a value of $1.23, and Autolus' market cap valuation stands at ~$343m.

Let's consider some headline figures:

Aucatzyl earned $23.3m of product revenues in Q4 2025, and $74.3m for the full-year. R&D expenses in Q4 amounted to $35.6m, and SG&A expenses $35.8m, and net loss was recorded as $(90.3m), compared to $(27.6m) in Q4 2024 (primarily due to an income expenses benefit of $49.7m). For 2025 as a whole, Autolus' net loss was $(288m), compared to $(221m) in the prior year. R&D expenses came to $118m, SG&A $132m, and cost of sales was recorded as $96.4m.

Guidance for 2026 has been provided for product revenues of $120m - $135m, with a "shift to positive gross margin." The company adds that:

Based on current operating plans, including anticipated AUCATZYL® net revenues, Autolus expects that its current and projected cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities will be sufficient to fund the Company’s operations into Q4 2027.

Today's press release also highlights upcoming milestones, as per the table below:

Analysis: Who'd Be A Cell Therapy Developer?

On the FDA website are listed ~50 approved cell therapies, however, many are quite obscure stem cell therapies, others more gene therapy than cell therapy (Elevidys, Zolgensma), and my research suggests that in 2025, only three bona fide cell therapies, Gilead Sciences' (GILD) Yescarta, Bristol Myers Squibb's (BMY) Breyanzi, and Johnson & Johnson's (JNJ) Carvykti, all indicated for cancers of the blood, earned "blockbuster" (>$1bn) revenues in 2025.

That underscores that, although they can be effective, and command high list prices, cell therapies are not necessarily major revenues drivers, and the treatment regime - collecting patients cells, transporting them to a lab and genetically engineering them to target proteins over-expressed by cancer cells, and then reinfusing back into patients - can be harsh on patients.

As such, many companies that have successfully developed cell therapies have struggled to make them commercially viable - the likes of Bluebird Bio and Adaptimmune have been taken private after running into financial difficulties, while other companies - Iovance Biotherapeutics (IOVA), for example - whose product Amtagvi is strictly speaking a tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) therapy - are heavily loss-making.

Despite securing approval for Aucatzyl in November 2024, Autolus stock is down 80% on a five-year basis, and down >40% year-to-date, despite a strong efficacy profile. The mechanism of action of the drug is shown below (source: Autolus presentation):

And the data that helped secure approval for the therapy is shown below:

Cell therapies typically treat very sick patients, and can be very toxic to patients, while durability of response is a common issue with the drug class also. With that said, Autolus has shared data showing that at 24 months, patients overall survival probability after being treated with Aucatzyl was 46%.

Autolus believes its addressable patient population is ~1,600 patients with r/r B-ALL, with an additional ~400 pediatric patients - the company is running a study in pediatric patients and reported the following updates today:

Preliminary data from the CATULUS Phase 1 trial of obe-cel in pediatric relapsed or refractory (r/r) B-ALL patients were presented at the American Society of Hematology (ASH) Annual Meeting in December 2025. Obe-cel demonstrated high remission rates in pediatric patients with high-risk r/r B-ALL with overall response rate (ORR) of 95.5%. Low rates of high-grade cytokine release syndrome (CRS) and immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome (ICANS) were observed, consistent with obe-cel’s adult safety profile. The Phase 2 portion of the trial is underway and Autolus expects to report data at the end of 2027.

Obe-Cel In Autoimmune Indications

Many cell therapy companies have been pivoting into treating autoimmune diseases, buoyed by a landmark study which showed patients with lupus being successfully treated.

Autolus is running studies of obe-cel in lupus nephritis and progressive multiple sclerosis. A Phase 1 study in lupus showed that (according to Autolus Q4 results press release) all patients show deep B-cell depletion after infusion, suggesting an immune reset. The company says it is aligned with the FDA on a Phase 2 trial design, which is now enrolling patients and is expected to read out data in 2028. In MS, Autolus has dosed a first patient in a Phase 1 study, and says it will report initial data at the end of this year.

Markets such as MS and Lupus are an order of magnitude greater than R/R B-ALL - ~25k - 35k in refractory lupus, and ~300k in MS, Autolus estimates. Clearly, a successful outcome in either study could result in the company's stock soaring in value. Other companies developing cell therapies for autoimmune - Cabaletta Bio (CABA), Kyverna Therapeutics (KYTX), and Artiva Biotherapeutics (ARTV), for example, have found the going tricky, although we may note their share prices are up 70%, >200%, and 30% on a one-year basis.

Looking Ahead: Autolus Faces A Tricky Path - I'm Not Investing But I Am Watching

Autolus reported $104m cash and $197m of marketable securities as of the end of 2025, which as mentioned is expected to last until the end of 2027.

Doubtless, Autolus will be hopeful that its long-term CARLYSLE date (systemic lupus erythematosus) and BOBCAT ("MS") study data due this year will be positive, drive the share price up, and allow the company to raise further funding at a competitive price (as of the end of Q3 2025, its accumulated deficit stood at ~$1.3bn).

My experience covering Autolus shows that the company has been able to generate generally strong clinical data - for example, Aucatzyl was not approved with a risk evaluation and mitigation strategy ("REMS"), handing it a competitive edge over Gilead's Tecartus and Novartis' (NVS), Kymriah, its main competitors (both cell therapies), which earned a combined >$700m of revenues next year.

Therefore, I believe that Autolus can meet its revenue targets for 2025, however funding does remain a concern - as per the company's Q3 10-Q filing:

We expect our expenses to increase substantially in connection with our ongoing activities, particularly as we begin to market and sell AUCATZYL, operate our new commercial manufacturing facility and advance the preclinical activities and clinical trials of our other product candidates.

All things considered then, as with any biotech with a valuation <$500m, we must acknowledge that any investment into the company does carry a significant level of risk, and note that the funding runway does not extend beyond the end of next year as things stand.

As discussed above, risks to Autolus' business include Aucatzyl struggling to grow revenues in a field where there is direct competition from rival cell therapies marketed and sold by well-resourced Pharma companies, that pediatric ALL, MS and SLE study data disappoints, and that funding is exhausted, while acknowledging the track record of similar companies, some of which have been unable to successfully commercialize cell therapies that showed in studies that they were both safe and effective.

On a more positive note, Aucatzyl may be able to establish itself as the standard of care in r/r B-ALL in 2026, and outperform guidance, potentially easing the pressure on the company in terms of funding, and if its good clinical study track record transmits to autoimmune disease treatment, and end-of year data wows the market, stock has every chance of doubling or even tripling in value.

Personally, I am going to award Autolus stock another Hold rating as the risk / reward profile of the company is slightly too challenging in my view, but I am intrigued to see how the data catalysts play out and hope the company is able to sustain itself financially and operationally for many more years.

data.title: Why Strait Of Hormuz Restrictions Could Have Far-Reaching Impact
data.description:
With movement in the Strait of Hormuz being heavily restricted by Iran, are investors underestimating the true scale of the disruption? Phil Davis, Founder of Philstockworld.com, breaks down why the impact could extend far beyond oil prices and how investors should be thinking about a prolonged period of uncertainty.

Transcript

Kim Parlee: As the conflict in the Middle East continues, a key focus for markets remain the Strait of Hormuz and whether it's open or closed. About 20% of the world's oil shipments travel through that area, and Iran is heavily restricting its use. And that, of course, has big implications for energy prices, inflation, and global growth.

On today's show, we're going to be looking at how investors could be positioning in this environment. And who better to provide some of that insight that one of our favorite guests, Phil Davis, founder of Philsstockworld.com.

Phil, it is always great to see you. How are you?

Phil Davis: I'm great. Kim, how are you doing?

Kim Parlee: I'm doing well. I'm doing well. We're happy to talk to you today. Always appreciate your thoughts on what's happening. And boy, is there are a lot happening.

I understand that you believe that the market right now is underestimating the real impact of the Strait of Hormuz crisis. Maybe so walk us through what you're seeing and why you feel that way.

Phil Davis: OK. Well, the thing you said right at the top-- you said 20 million barrels, and that's 20% of the world's supply. That is, I believe, an incorrect way of looking at it, and we hear it over and over and over again. But if you think about it, America, 13 million barrels a day. Canada, 5 million barrels a day. Mexico, a couple million barrels a day. Venezuela, 1.5 million barrels a day.

So, on our side of the world, we're producing 22, 23 million barrels of oil a day. So the global supply that we're talking about is less our oil. And that's a very, very big deal. You have to look at it that way, because they've only got 60 million barrels-- or, I'm sorry, 80 million barrels total left after we take out ours. We're not shipping it over there.

So, the amount of available oil in the area is 80. And, frankly, then you've got to take out China, and you've got to take out Russia's oil, which is-- Russia's oil is for Russia, China's oil for China, for the most part. So there's another 20 million barrels that's not available to the global supply.

That leaves 60 million barrels of total global supply, and 20 million-- which is a third-- is cut off from the Strait of Hormuz. So it's devastating for the countries that rely on that oil, like Japan and India and Germany-- major countries that are going to grind to a halt for lack of oil.

Kim Parlee: Yeah, no. Again, when you get out of, I'll call, the economic averages, so to speak, and start to focus on where it is, it's a big difference. You talked about Japan. You talked about India. What are you hearing about on the ground, what it's like for parts of Asia that are dealing with that severe restriction of oil?

Phil Davis: Yeah, they're physically constrained. Because the missing oil, it's not quite 20 million barrels a day because some is finding alternate routes and so on and so forth. But whatever oil is not getting out is permanently gone from the supply. You're basically taking the supply chain and you've cut or you've drained out, like we're doing with the Strategic Petroleum Reserves-- you're physically draining away any excess capacity, which makes you much tighter the next time there's some kind of an emergency. And this emergency is sure not over.

So, countries are physically having to shut down and conserve. Now, that's, to some extent, demand destruction-- which is, we know, another factor that we have to take into account down the road. But Japan is tapping their reserves. South Korea is tapping their reserves. And they have to actually physically shut down. Like, they're saying these planes can't run seven days a week. They have to shut down two days a week.

Kim Parlee: So, huge impact on parts of Asia, severe impact, I will say, on that. When you bring it back to then North America and Europe, is it much less so? Is it overstated then there? Or what are you seeing there?

Phil Davis: Well, the funny thing about America-- and you guys too, though. You export oil, you're a net exporter of oil. We're a net-- we import oil. We produce 13 million barrels, we import another 3 or 4 million barrels. But the funny thing is, we export 5 million barrels a day of refined products. So, a lot of what people think of as North American consumption or American consumption is actually just oil that we're taking to our refineries and turning it into products and then sending them out of the country. We're not actually consuming it.

The other thing is-- I'm sure you know what force majeure is. Force majeure is the ability to declare contracts voided because of circumstances like a war. So, in many parts of the world, they're declaring force majeure. The oil is undeliverable due to the war.

We don't have that. Canada and America can't say to Mexico, "We're declaring force majeure because of the war." The war's on the other side of the world. So we're less affected in that way than the Asian countries and the European countries that are having contracts canceled. And that also impacts their delivery prices because they have to go to the spot market, and the spot market in Asia is like $150 a barrel now.

Kim Parlee: What are you watching as you look ahead? There's a lot of headlines. It can be very confusing to people sometimes. It's all going badly. Sometimes it's all going well. And it changes minute by minute. What are you watching in terms of, I'll say-- I know you think that we're one drone strike away from some pretty bad things happening. I also know you're watching second-order effects. So let's just talk about those a bit.

Phil Davis: Well, the second-order effects-- if you go back to the strait, the second-order effect is that it's too risky to send ships through the strait. Therefore, the insurance companies are saying if you're even going to send a ship through, it's going to be astronomically expensive to insure it, or we won't insure it at all. And that effectively grounds the ship, because an oil-laden ship is effectively a bomb. And all it takes is one drone hitting the ship and the whole thing goes up. So you lose the shipment, you lose the ship, et cetera, et cetera. It's not worth the risk.

Not to mention the fact, where do you get the guys who want to get on that ship and crew it across the Strait of Hormuz? Are you going to really compensate them for the risk to their lives? They're not army guys. They're sailors.

So, there's knock-on effects like that. And then, oh, an interesting one is they've got the LNG tankers tied up in the Strait of Hormuz. They're not making their crossings like they're supposed to. And therefore, natural gas in part of Texas-- not the main terminal, but it's one of the subterminals in Texas-- is actually selling for negative money because they have nowhere to put it.

So they pay you to take away the natural gas. And meanwhile, if you could somehow magically get it over to Japan, they'll pay a fortune for it.

Kim Parlee: Yeah. Well, I guess this is where we're understanding the geographic importance of where resources are yet again. Listen, I've only got about 10 seconds here before I have to go to break. But just quickly, how do you think investors need to think about this environment?

Phil Davis: I think you have to embrace the uncertainty. And we've tremendously pared back to cash in our portfolios because it is too uncertain, it's too risky. You can't trust what you're hearing from the politicians. Everybody is telling a different story. It's just a nightmare to try to figure out what's actually happening.

data.title: AbbVie: Strong 2026 Outlook, Expect Dividend Increases
data.description:
Shares of AbbVie Inc. (ABBV) are down approximately 10% since my October 2025 update, in which I outlined the strong commercial progress and the company’s emerging efforts in the obesity market with the in-licensed amylin analog candidate GUBamy, now called ABBV-295. I was also cautious about the near-term share price performance expectations as the valuation reached 10-year highs, and that is what has essentially happened since then, although worsening market conditions did most of the work.

I am still not expecting miracles from AbbVie in the near term, but I continue to see it as well-positioned for long-term growth. I also expect dividend increases to accelerate in the following years as the company returns to stronger top- and bottom-line growth.

Expect Double-Digit Revenue Growth This Year With Skyrizi And Rinvoq Doing A Lot Of The Heavy Lifting Again

AbbVie had a strong end to 2025 with $61.16 billion in total revenue. This number came slightly ahead of the $61.1 billion midpoint of my revenue estimate range, which I have been increasing throughout the year.

Since my last update, we have seen more of the same, with Skyrizi and Rinvoq being responsible for most of the revenue upside versus expectations.

I expect to see more of the same this year, with both products delivering 20%+ revenue growth, driven by continued adoption in existing indications.

Management guided for $67 billion in revenue this year, a 9.5% increase over 2025, and I expect to see double-digit topline growth this year. My preliminary revenue estimate range for 2026 is $66.7-68.9 billion, with the $67.8 billion midpoint being $800 million ahead of AbbVie’s guidance.

The difference is coming from a $1 billion higher revenue estimate for the immunology segment (Skyrizi and Rinvoq) and slightly higher expectations for the neuroscience segment, offset by lower expectations for the oncology and aesthetics segments. Guidance for eye care and combined other products was not provided, but we can get it by calculating the difference to the sum of the largest segments, and I am in line with this combined difference.

I expect adjusted EPS in 2026 to be above $15 per share versus the company’s $14.37-14.57 guidance range, but this does not include estimates for IPR&D expenses that the company incurs throughout the year.

In the first quarter, AbbVie guided for $14.7 billion in total revenue, and I expect nearly $15 billion at the midpoint of my estimate range, with the same upside drivers (immunology and neuroscience) and downside drivers (oncology and aesthetics).

I expect adjusted EPS in the first quarter to be north of $3.05 versus the company’s $2.97-3.01 guidance range, driven by the expected revenue outperformance.

Expect Dividend Increases And More Business Development From Growing Revenues And Cash Flows

I have to give credit to AbbVie for increasing the dividend throughout the Humira erosion period. The quarterly dividend has increased from $1.41 at the start of 2022 to $1.73 at the start of 2026, a 5.4% dividend growth rate during this period.

With Humira erosion in the rearview mirror, the digestion of larger acquisitions from the last few years, and a strong top- and bottom-line growth outlook for 2026 and the following years, I expect to see acceleration in dividend increases in the following years.

AbbVie expects to generate $18.5 billion in free cash flow this year, and I expect business development activity to pick up, and I expect the obesity pipeline to be the main area the company will focus on.

Obesity Pipeline Needs Reinforcements - The Undifferentiated Amylin Analog Is Insufficient

AbbVie recently reported additional phase 1 results of ABBV-295 in obese patients. Weight loss ranged from 7.7% to 9.7% at week 12 when ABBV-295 was dosed weekly, 7.8% when dosed every other week, and 6.8% when it was dosed monthly. The results for the bi-weekly and monthly doses look better at week 13 with 9.7% and 7.9% reductions, respectively.

We are yet to see the safety data, as all we got was AbbVie saying that gastrointestinal side effects were the most common side effect and that they were mostly mild.

With the caveats of cross-trial comparisons, this puts ABBV-295 in the middle of the amylin pack, as it achieved better weight loss than Roche’s (RHHBY) and Zealand Pharma’s (ZLDPF) petrelintide, which had up to 8.6% weight loss at week 16 and which delivered disappointing weight loss data in the phase 2b trial recently, and ABBV-295 delivered worse weight loss than Eli Lilly’s eloralintide, which achieved 8.5% to 11.3% weight loss at week 12 (page 25 of the 2025 ADA presentation).

Every other week and monthly dosing looks achievable, but this is also unlikely to be a differentiating feature, as I expect Pfizer’s amylin analog, which it gained through last year’s acquisition of Metsera, to be competitive with eloralintide and ABBV-295 on efficacy and with ABBV-295 on dosing frequency with monthly and potentially less frequent dosing.

Both Pfizer and Eli Lilly, as well as Novo Nordisk (NVO), are better positioned than AbbVie at the moment, as they have plenty of other drug class candidates to combine with their amylin analogs, and Novo Nordisk already has a late-stage dual GLP-1/amylin agonist called amycretin (both subcutaneous and oral versions). In the phase 2 trial, amycretin delivered up to 22% weight loss without a plateau at week 36.

As such, a standalone amylin analog is unlikely to create significant shareholder value, especially for a company of AbbVie’s size. It is a good foundation molecule, but it needs combination partners.

I must say I expected more from AbbVie’s business development team, as it has been a year since the deal with Gubra, and time is not AbbVie’s friend here, as the field is evolving rapidly and combination strategies are critical to gain considerable market share. Yes, AbbVie can in-license or acquire additional assets, but it is losing time by not having candidates from other drug classes to combine with ABBV-295.

Conclusion

AbbVie is going through a share price consolidation phase, and I do not see much that is wrong with the company. Quite the contrary, I continue to see it as well-positioned for long-term value creation, and I expect to see healthy increases in the quarterly dividend in the following years.

The only meaningful criticism I have is the lack of urgency on the obesity side, as it has been a year since the foundational in-licensing deal with Gubra. I would like to see the company do more deals this year.

data.title: Dow Jones And U.S. Stock Market Outlook - Stocks Reach New Lows As War Goes On
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Equities are feeling the intense heat of the swift return to risk aversion.

US stock benchmarks have dropped to new lows since the conflict began, with the Nasdaq and S&P 500 under the most severe pressure (while the Dow Jones is not there yet).

Across the board, major indices are down 1% and more for the session, with the pump-fake from Monday now turning against itself: Any hopes for a quick peace deal have proven short-lived, and the market's previous easing in sentiment was merely a timid pause.

Bulls who bought that dip are now paying the consequences.

The cause: A classic, violent rebound in energy markets: Oil continues to surge, with Brent crude pushing back above the critical $110 per barrel mark. This spike in energy costs is logically reawakening inflation fears and dragging down risk assets – But this time, gold is actually faring better.

The particular turn comes from the fact that traders are actively pricing in the risk of a severe escalation over the weekend: the impending arrival of a 4,500-strong Marine fleet in the Middle East is adding to the immense geopolitical anxiety.

Investors are tracking headlines by the minute, waiting to see if an anticipated Iranian counterproposal to a security deal actually materializes.

As traditional markets close, all eyes will turn to Bitcoin (BTC-USD) over the weekend. Because it trades 24/7, it will act as the primary real-time barometer for global sentiment.

Let's spot where today's rough price action is heading by looking at today’s intraday charts and trading levels for the major US indexes: the Dow Jones, Nasdaq, and S&P 500.

Current Session's Stock Heatmap

Today's selloff is surprisingly pulled by the largest cap stocks and isn't so widespread, with energy stocks naturally appreciating from the bounce in commodities, but utilities and industrials are somehow rising despite the broad pessimistic mood – A turn to defensives?

Among the laggards, some large-cap finance stocks are getting hammered, including JPMorgan (JPM), Visa (V), and Mastercard (MA), while virtually all the Mag 7s are also dropping, with Amazon (AMZN) being the worst performer of them all.

Dow Jones 4H Chart and Trading Levels

DJIA has officially re-entered its bear channel after a failed attempt to break above – bulls could not hold the 4H 50-period MA, rejecting the major 46,600 intraday level signaled throughout our analysis from this week.

Now 300 points shy of its war lows, the index will face pressure ahead if the narrative doesn't get better, particularly with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq breaking their own lows.

A short-term target for the current selloff would be the lows of the channel around 44,500-44,600.

Nasdaq 4H Chart and Trading Levels

Nasdaq is now testing a pivotal support that preceded the rally to its end-2025 records.
